question: When does the right of private defence of the body commence, and how long does it continue?
generated_answer:
The right of private defence of the body commences as soon as a reasonable apprehension of danger to the body arises from an attempt or threat to commit the offence, though the offence may not have been committed [Chunk-ID: d3d93d06-b511-4735-9b84-6085daecad6e]. 
It continues as long as such apprehension of danger to the body continues [Chunk-ID: d3d93d06-b511-4735-9b84-6085daecad6e]. 
This means that the right of private defence of the body starts when there is a reasonable fear of harm to the body and lasts until that fear is no longer present [Chunk-ID: d3d93d06-b511-4735-9b84-6085daecad6e].

question: What punishment applies to abetment of an offence when the Sanhita makes no express provision for punishing that specific abetment?
generated_answer: When the Sanhita makes no express provision for punishing a specific abetment, the punishment for the abetment is the same as the punishment for the offence that was abetted, if the act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment [Chunk-ID: 3a452a16-b9c6-4177-bd3d-bafce7713aaf]. Additionally, if the person abetted does the act with a different intention or knowledge from that of the abettor, the abettor is punished with the punishment provided for the offence that would have been committed if the act had been done with the intention or knowledge of the abettor [Chunk-ID: 3a452a16-b9c6-4177-bd3d-bafce7713aaf]. Furthermore, if the offence abetted is punishable with death or imprisonment for life and is not committed, the abettor is punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and is also liable to a fine [Chunk-ID: f69e6aef-3594-43c8-86e9-415389ec638f].

question: How does the BNS define 'criminal conspiracy'?
generated_answer: According to the context chunk 1 [Chunk-ID: 40abf7d3-8696-4424-88ed-e3ba45bfe867], the definition of 'criminal conspiracy' is provided in section 61, which states that when two or more persons agree with the common object to do, or cause to be done, an illegal act or an act which is not illegal by illegal means, such an agreement is designated a criminal conspiracy [Chunk-ID: 40abf7d3-8696-4424-88ed-e3ba45bfe867]. question: If a person intends to kill one individual but instead unintentionally causes the death of a different person, how is the resulting culpable homicide classified?
generated_answer: If a person intends to kill one individual but instead unintentionally causes the death of a different person, the resulting culpable homicide is classified as culpable homicide by causing death of person other than person whose death was intended [Chunk-ID: cb540380-0fc6-40ad-bf08-4db5f0ea2a0a]. According to section 102, the culpable homicide committed by the offender is of the description of which it would have been if he had caused the death of the person whose death he intended or knew himself to be likely to cause [Chunk-ID: cb540380-0fc6-40ad-bf08-4db5f0ea2a0a]. This means that the offender's intention or knowledge regarding the original intended victim is used to determine the classification of the culpable homicide, even though the actual victim was different [Chunk-ID: cb540380-0fc6-40ad-bf08-4db5f0ea2a0a].
